summary:
Tell Aswad is a prehistoric site in the Damascus Basin of Syria, located on the eastern side of the Anti-Lebanon range, occupied from approximately 8800 to 6800 BCE. It is among the earliest known sites in the southern Levant with clear evidence for the cultivation of emmer wheat and einkorn, placing it among the founding communities of Near Eastern agriculture alongside sites such as [[event:1312|Netiv Hagdud]] and Jericho.

Excavated by French archaeologists in multiple seasons, Tell Aswad yielded charred grain assemblages showing both morphologically wild and transitional forms of wheat and barley, reflecting the early stages of domestication when cultivated populations had not yet fully diverged from their wild ancestors. The site also produced evidence for the management of cattle, pigs, and caprines (sheep and goats). Its location in an inland basin away from the core Levantine corridor suggests that agricultural practices spread quickly from initial centers of domestication across a wide range of ecological settings in the early Neolithic period.
